title: Fortoon - Cartoon Platform
short: A full-stack digital cartoon read / write platform
summary: Built a scalable marketplace experience for virtual goods and gaming services, combining a polished storefront with transactional backend systems.
overview: Fortoon is a responsive web platform that enables users to read, publish, and purchase digital comics while providing a community space for interaction between readers and creators.
features: Storefront with catalog browsing and search for virtual goods and gaming services
Concurrency-safe order processing with transactional consistency
Integrated payment gateways and user verification
Admin dashboard for order, inventory, and user management
architecture: The backend exposes a REST API built on Next.js, backed by MySQL for durable transactional data and Redis for session state and hot-path caching. Order writes are wrapped in database transactions to keep inventory and payment state consistent under concurrent checkouts. The React.js frontend consumes the API directly, with a separate admin surface reusing the same service layer.
challenges: Keeping inventory and order state consistent when multiple buyers checked out the same limited-stock item simultaneously
Integrating third-party payment providers while keeping failure and retry paths safe for the buyer and the ledger
Applying OWASP-aligned controls across authentication, input handling, and admin access
process: Modeled the order and payment domain before writing any UI, to get transaction boundaries right early
Built the admin tooling in parallel with the storefront so operational gaps surfaced during development, not after launch
Layered in caching and query tuning once the core transactional flow was correct and covered by tests
lessons: Transactional correctness is cheaper to design up front than to retrofit once real traffic hits shared inventory
Admin tooling is part of the product, not an afterthought — it shaped several API decisions

title: Marketplace Website
short: A full-stack marketplace for digital goods and gaming services with payments, concurrency, and admin tooling.
summary: Built a scalable marketplace experience for virtual goods and gaming services, combining a polished storefront with transactional backend systems.
overview: A full-stack marketplace platform for virtual goods and gaming-related services, built with React.js, Express.js, MySQL, and Redis. The platform handles the full lifecycle of a digital storefront: browsing, checkout, payment, and post-purchase order management.
features: Storefront with catalog browsing and search for virtual goods and gaming services
Concurrency-safe order processing with transactional consistency
Integrated payment gateways and user verification
Admin dashboard for order, inventory, and user management
architecture: The backend exposes a REST API built on Express.js, backed by MySQL for durable transactional data and Redis for session state and hot-path caching. Order writes are wrapped in database transactions to keep inventory and payment state consistent under concurrent checkouts. The React.js frontend consumes the API directly, with a separate admin surface reusing the same service layer.
challenges: Keeping inventory and order state consistent when multiple buyers checked out the same limited-stock item simultaneously
Integrating third-party payment providers while keeping failure and retry paths safe for the buyer and the ledger
Applying OWASP-aligned controls across authentication, input handling, and admin access
process: Modeled the order and payment domain before writing any UI, to get transaction boundaries right early
Built the admin tooling in parallel with the storefront so operational gaps surfaced during development, not after launch
Layered in caching and query tuning once the core transactional flow was correct and covered by tests
lessons: Transactional correctness is cheaper to design up front than to retrofit once real traffic hits shared inventory
Admin tooling is part of the product, not an afterthought — it shaped several API decisions

title: AI Image Generation Platform
short: An AI-powered product for transforming text prompts into customized artwork with usage credits and dashboards.
summary: Delivered an AI image generation experience with prompt-driven generation, subscription-style credit handling, and a business-friendly admin layer.
overview: A prompt-to-image product built with Next.js on the frontend, connected to the ChatGPT Sora API for generation. The platform wraps AI generation in a usable product layer: accounts, credits, and history, plus internal tools for the team running it.
features: Prompt-driven image generation against the ChatGPT Sora API
Credit-based usage system with balance tracking per account
User authentication and generation history
Internal admin tools for transaction tracking and user oversight
architecture: The Next.js frontend handles routing, authentication state, and the generation UI, calling a Node.js service layer that proxies requests to the Sora API and persists generation history and credit balances in MySQL. Credit deduction and generation requests are coordinated so a failed generation doesn't consume a user's balance.
challenges: Handling variable AI generation latency without making the UI feel broken or unresponsive
Keeping credit accounting accurate when generation requests fail or time out
Designing an admin layer non-technical staff could use to track usage and transactions
process: Prototyped the generation flow first against the raw API to understand latency and failure modes
Built the credit system around those failure modes rather than the happy path
Added admin visibility once the core product loop was stable
lessons: Third-party AI APIs need explicit timeout and retry handling baked into the product logic, not just the network layer
Usage-based billing logic benefits from being designed alongside the feature it meters, not bolted on after

title: Deekub
short: A location-based social platform for nightlife venues with real-time interactions and discovery features.
summary: Created a mobile-first social platform connecting nightlife venues in Bangkok through location-aware experiences and real-time features.
overview: A cross-platform mobile app that connects users with nightlife venues in Bangkok through location-aware discovery and real-time social features, backed by a Go service layer built with clean architecture principles.
features: Location-aware venue discovery and check-ins
Real-time messaging between users
Cross-platform mobile client built with Flutter
Media storage and push notifications
architecture: The backend is written in Go using clean architecture and dependency injection, separating domain logic from delivery (REST, WebSocket) and infrastructure (PostgreSQL, Redis, media storage). The Flutter client talks to the API over REST for standard operations and WebSocket for real-time messaging and presence, with Docker-based deployment pipelines for both.
challenges: Keeping real-time messaging reliable across flaky mobile network conditions
Structuring the Go backend so domain logic stayed testable and independent of delivery mechanisms
Handling location data efficiently for proximity-based venue discovery
process: Established the clean-architecture boundaries in Go before adding real-time features, to avoid coupling domain logic to WebSocket handling
Built the Flutter client against a mocked API first so mobile and backend work could proceed in parallel
Hardened deployment with Docker once core features were feature-complete
lessons: Separating domain logic from transport (REST vs WebSocket) early made adding real-time features far less disruptive later
Mobile-first products need explicit design for intermittent connectivity, not just happy-path networking

title: Inventory System
short: Custom web products for business workflows, dashboards, and internal operations.
summary: Delivered custom business-platform solutions with secure dashboards, workflows, and reliable deployment setups.
overview: A set of internal and client-facing dashboards and workflow tools built with Vue.js and Spring Boot, focused on role-based access, operational visibility, and dependable deployment.
features: Role-based access control across internal and client-facing views
Workflow management dashboards for day-to-day operations
Monitoring and automation to reduce manual operational work
Nginx-fronted deployment on Linux infrastructure
architecture: Spring Boot exposes the business logic and role-based authorization as a REST API backed by PostgreSQL, with Vue.js consuming it across separate internal and client-facing dashboards. Nginx sits in front of the application as a reverse proxy on Linux, with Docker used to keep deployments repeatable.
challenges: Modeling role-based access so permissions stayed simple to reason about as the number of workflows grew
Keeping internal and client-facing dashboards consistent while serving different audiences
Making deployments repeatable across environments without manual intervention
process: Mapped out roles and permissions with stakeholders before building any dashboard screens
Built the API and access-control layer first, then layered the Vue.js dashboards on top
Automated the deployment path with Docker and Nginx once the application stabilized
lessons: Getting role and permission modeling right early avoids painful rework as new workflows get added
Shared backend, separate frontends is an effective pattern when internal and client audiences need different views of the same data
